title: Wyoming
text:
790
40%
7
People In Prison PastParole Eligibility*(2023 Projection TBD)
Prison Population IncarceratedPast Parole Eligibility(2023 Projection TBD)
Parole BoardMembers





*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and excludes people with life sentences and sentences less than one year.


Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Wyoming, sentencing courts have wide discretion to vary the degree of indeterminacy in a sentence, with minimums allowed to range up to 90 percent of the maximum. Good time credits can achieve a one-third reduction (15 days per month) in a mandatory release date and advance parole eligibility.¹







In 2019, 40 percent of people in prison were currently past their parole eligibility. Another 60 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2019.





From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 27 percent.












Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2019, 75 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic.






In 2019, 88 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.






In 2019, 32 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old.














In 2019, 51 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 49 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for rape or sexual assault (30%) and drug (23%) offenses.











In 2019, 40 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 5 and 9.9 years.











Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (90 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 90 for those whose most serious offense is violent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴







¹ Kevin R. Reitz, Bree Crye, and Edward E. Rhine, Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size: State Report: Wyoming (Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, August 2022), https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2023-03/wyoming_doi_report_8_4_22.pdf.² US Bureau of Justice Statistics., “National Corrections Reporting Program NCRP Series” (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research, 2022), accessed October 2, 2024, https://www.icpsr.umich.edu/web/NACJD/series/38.³ Kevin Reitz et al., American Prison-Release Systems: Indeterminacy in Sentencing and the Control of Prison Population Size, Final Report (Minneapolis, Minnesota: Robina Institute, University of Minnesota, 2022), accessed October 1, 2024, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-05/american_prison-release_systems.pdf, 36-43.⁴ US Bureau of Justice Statistics, “National Prisoner Statistics, 1978-2022” (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research), accessed January 10, 2024, https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38871.v1.“Wyoming Department of Corrections Population Report for Quarter Ending 2023Q4,” Wyoming Department of Corrections, accessed October 1, 2024, https://drive.google.com/file/d/1R2octURWUtZ9MUPR4nVsHBPyS0j8RwGp/view.
Data analysis was conducted using information from the National Corrections Reporting Program and the National Prisoner Statistics Program. See “National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP),” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp; “Prisoners - Statistical Tables,” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, https://bjs.ojp.gov/library/publications/list?series_filter=Prisoners.





From 2010 to 2022, the prison population increased 1 percent, changing from 2,112 in 2010 to 2,123 in 2022.












Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 76 percent of people in prison were White, non-Hispanic.






In 2020, 89 percent of people in prison were male.






In 2020, 30 percent of people in prison were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old.














In 2020, 24 percent of people in prison were incarcerated for rape or sexual assault offenses.











In 2020, 33 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 5 and 9.9 years.









From 2010 to 2020, people released from prison increased 41 percent, dropping from 787 in 2010 to 1,110 in 2020.





In 2020, 62 percent of parole-eligible people released in Wyoming were released past their parole eligibility year, which is an increase of 77 percent from 2010.











Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. In 2019, 72 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases.












Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2019, 74 percent of people released from prison were White, non-Hispanic.






In 2019, 82 percent of people released from prison were male.






In 2019, 39 percent of people released from prison were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old.














In 2019, 29 percent of people released from prison were incarcerated for drug offenses.










Race and EthnicitySex



In 2019, Hispanic people were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 1.2 times higher than White people, when accounting for prison population sizes in Wyoming.Hispanic RRI should be interpreted with caution due to inconsistencies in how each state collects and reports data on ethnicity.




In 2019, males  were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 1.2 times higher than females, when accounting for prison population sizes in Wyoming.











Race and EthnicitySex


In 2019, Black people who were still incarcerated spent on average 0.1 less years past parole eligibility, and Hispanic people who were still incarcerated spent on average 0.5 less years past parole eligibility compared to White people.

The chart below shows the average time spent in prison past parole eligibility by offense type and race and ethnicity in 2020. The largest disparity was observed among drug offenses, where Black people spent on average 1.9 more years in prison compared to White people.






In 2019, females who were still incarcerated spent on average 1.1 less years past parole eligibility compared to males in Wyoming.


Average Years in Prison Past Parole Eligibility

Male

The chart below shows the average time spent in prison past parole eligibility by offense type and sex in 2020. The largest disparity was observed among murder or nonnegligent manslaughter offenses, where males spent on average 1.1 more years in prison compared to females.















Race and EthnicitySex




In 2020, Black people were incarcerated in state prison at a rate 5.6 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Wyoming.

In 2020, Hispanic people were incarcerated in state prison at a rate 1.5 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Wyoming.Hispanic RRI should be interpreted with caution due to inconsistencies in how each state collects and reports data on ethnicity.

Race and EthnicitySex


In 2019, Black people released spent on average 0.1 less years in prison, and Hispanic people released spent on average 0.5 less years in prison compared to White people.




The chart below shows the average time spent in prison past parole eligibility by offense type and race and ethnicity in 2020.






In 2019, females released spent on average 0.7 less years in prison compared to males in Wyoming.




The chart below shows the average time served in prison by offense type and sex in 2020. The largest disparity was observed among murder or nonnegligent manslaughter offenses, where males spent on average 6.4 more years in prison compared to females.














Interpret small numbers with caution, as they may introduce variability and lead to less reliable estimates. We do not report on people of “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” because the numbers are generally smaller, and disparities analyses require sufficient sample sizes to produce meaningful comparisons. In the NCRP data, people of “Other race(s)” may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Due to the small numbers in these groups, reporting could lead to unreliable or misleading results.
The Relative Rate Index (RRI) is a tool used to compare the rates at which different groups experience incarceration, parole eligibility, or other criminal justice system outcomes. In this report, we use the RRI to highlight disparities in parole release and incarceration across race, ethnicity, and sex. An RRI greater than one means that the group experiences the event at a higher rate, while an RRI less than one means they experience it at a lower rate.
State data broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. The U.S. Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Utah, sentencing judges have no discretion to set or influence the actual durations of the prison sentences they impose for each crime. “Judicial” maximum prison sentences must be fixed at the same level as the statutory maximum penalty for the offense of conviction. Any applicable minimum terms to parole-release eligibility are also preset by statute. Parole release dates are determined by the parole board.¹







In 2019, 29 percent of people in prison were currently past their parole eligibility. Another 71 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2019.





From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 11 percent.












Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2019, 65 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic.






In 2019, 91 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.






In 2019, 33 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 35 and 44 years old.














In 2019, 72 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 28 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for rape or sexual assault (33%) and property (11%) offenses.











In 2019, 64 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 and 24.9 years.











Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (11 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 11 for those whose most serious offense is violent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 13 percent, changing from 6,807 in 2010 to 5,911 in 2022.

From 2010 to 2020, people released from prison increased 34 percent, dropping from 3,108 in 2010 to 4,156 in 2020.





In 2020, 55 percent of parole-eligible people released in Utah were released past their parole eligibility year, which is an increase of 28 percent from 2010.

Race and EthnicitySex

Interpret small numbers with caution, as they may introduce variability and lead to less reliable estimates. We do not report on people of “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” because the numbers are generally smaller, and disparities analyses require sufficient sample sizes to produce meaningful comparisons. In the NCRP data, people of “Other race(s)” may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Due to the small numbers in these groups, reporting could lead to unreliable or misleading results.
The Relative Rate Index (RRI) is a tool used to compare the rates at which different groups experience incarceration, parole eligibility, or other criminal justice system outcomes. In this report, we use the RRI to highlight disparities in parole release and incarceration across race, ethnicity, and sex. An RRI greater than one means that the group experiences the event at a higher rate, while an RRI less than one means they experience it at a lower rate.
State data broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. The U.S. Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

title: Utah
text:
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Utah, sentencing judges have no discretion to set or influence the actual durations of the prison sentences they impose for each crime. “Judicial” maximum prison sentences must be fixed at the same level as the statutory maximum penalty for the offense of conviction. Any applicable minimum terms to parole-release eligibility are also preset by statute. Parole release dates are determined by the parole board.¹

title: Utah
text:
In 2019, 29 percent of people in prison were currently past their parole eligibility. Another 71 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2019.





From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 11 percent.

title: Utah
text:
Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2019, 65 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic.






In 2019, 91 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.






In 2019, 33 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 35 and 44 years old.

title: Utah
text: In 2019, 72 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 28 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for rape or sexual assault (33%) and property (11%) offenses.

title: Utah
text: In 2019, 64 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 and 24.9 years.

title: Utah
text: Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (11 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 11 for those whose most serious offense is violent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Tennessee, sentences under two years are released to probation automatically upon eligibility. There is a statutory staggered percentage formula (generally 30–60 percent of the maximum sentence) for parole-release eligibility, differentiated by sentencing “range” based on prior convictions. Starting in 2021, there is a growing list of 100-percent offenses.¹







In 2019, 60 percent of people in prison were currently past their parole eligibility. Another 40 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2019.





From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 10 percent.












Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2019, 56 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic.






In 2019, 90 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.






In 2019, 34 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old.














In 2019, 51 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 49 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for drug (22%) and property (21%) offenses.











In 2019, 37 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 and 24.9 years.











Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues and the state’s code.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (60 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 60 for those whose most serious offense is violent, and 85 percent for those with an offense type that might have special eligibility restrictions). Third, in Tennesee, the percentage of the max. sentence that corresponds to the min. sentence depends on previous sentences.Because data on previous terms was not published for 2018 and 2020, all cases were assumed to have previous sentences. Consequently, the rules-based estimated min. sentence was replaced by 60 percent of the max. sentence. All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues and the state’s code.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (60 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 60 for those whose most serious offense is violent, and 85 percent for those with an offense type that might have special eligibility restrictions). Third, in Tennesee, the percentage of the max. sentence that corresponds to the min. sentence depends on previous sentences.Because data on previous terms was not published for 2018 and 2020, all cases were assumed to have previous sentences. Consequently, the rules-based estimated min. sentence was replaced by 60 percent of the max. sentence. All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

title: Tennessee
text:
Interpret small numbers with caution, as they may introduce variability and lead to less reliable estimates. We do not report on people of “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” because the numbers are generally smaller, and disparities analyses require sufficient sample sizes to produce meaningful comparisons. In the NCRP data, people of “Other race(s)” may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Due to the small numbers in these groups, reporting could lead to unreliable or misleading results.
The Relative Rate Index (RRI) is a tool used to compare the rates at which different groups experience incarceration, parole eligibility, or other criminal justice system outcomes. In this report, we use the RRI to highlight disparities in parole release and incarceration across race, ethnicity, and sex. An RRI greater than one means that the group experiences the event at a higher rate, while an RRI less than one means they experience it at a lower rate.
State data broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. The U.S. Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

title: South Carolina

*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and excludes people with life sentences and sentences less than one year.


Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In South Carolina, most people who are incarcerated become eligible for discretionary parole release at 25 or 33 percent of the maximum sentence with the possibility of somewhat earlier eligibility through credit earnings of 20 days per month. “No parole” crimes are not eligible for parole until they have served 85 percent of the judicial sentence, with three days of credit per month.¹







In 2019, 45 percent of people in prison were currently past their parole eligibility. Another 52 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2019.





From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 2 percent.












Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2019, 62 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were Black, non-Hispanic.






In 2019, 95 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.






In 2019, 38 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old.














In 2019, 49 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 51 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for property (26%) and drug (20%) offenses.











In 2019, 55 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 and 24.9 years.











Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues and the state’s code.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the percentage of the max. sentence (25 percent for most people, and 85 percent for those with an offense type that might have special eligibility restrictions). Third, in South Carolina there is no parole for people with two or more violent offenses. Because data on previous terms was not published for 2018 and 2020, rules-based estimated PEY was removed for every one with a violent offense. All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

text:
Interpret small numbers with caution, as they may introduce variability and lead to less reliable estimates. We do not report on people of “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” because the numbers are generally smaller, and disparities analyses require sufficient sample sizes to produce meaningful comparisons. In the NCRP data, people of “Other race(s)” may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Due to the small numbers in these groups, reporting could lead to unreliable or misleading results.
The Relative Rate Index (RRI) is a tool used to compare the rates at which different groups experience incarceration, parole eligibility, or other criminal justice system outcomes. In this report, we use the RRI to highlight disparities in parole release and incarceration across race, ethnicity, and sex. An RRI greater than one means that the group experiences the event at a higher rate, while an RRI less than one means they experience it at a lower rate.
State data broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. The U.S. Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
Pennsylvania judges have discretion to set the durations of minimum terms to parole-release eligibility, which can be any length up to 50 percent of the maximum.¹







In 2019, 48 percent of people in prison were currently past their parole eligibility. Another 52 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2019.





From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 8 percent.












Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2019, 48 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic.






In 2019, 95 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.






In 2019, 35 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old.














In 2019, 61 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 39 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for rape or sexual assault (22%) and aggravated or simple assault (15%) offenses.











In 2019, 39 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 and 24.9 years.











Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues and the state’s code.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (50 percent). Second, the absolute min. sentence of when the most serious offense is non-violent and 25 yearsfor people with offenses of more than . years when the most serious offense is violent is computed. The previously computed rules-based estimate was replaced by the absolute min. sentence if the latter was shorter. Third, in Pennsylvania, people with previous terms have additional restrictions. Because data on previous terms was not published for 2018 and 2019, they were applied to everyoned. Thus, everyone with a max. sentence between 7 and 24 years assigned a min. sentence of 7 years and everyone with a max. sentence of 25 or more years was assigned a min. sentence of 25, if those minimums were greater than the previouslyassigned minimum. All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

In 2019, 26 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 74 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for drug (38%) and property (21%) offenses.











In 2019, 47 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 2 and 4.9 years.











Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the percentage of the max. sentence (0 percent for most people, and 85 percent for those with an offense type that might have special eligibility restrictions). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

Interpret small numbers with caution, as they may introduce variability and lead to less reliable estimates. We do not report on people of “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” because the numbers are generally smaller, and disparities analyses require sufficient sample sizes to produce meaningful comparisons. In the NCRP data, people of “Other race(s)” may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Due to the small numbers in these groups, reporting could lead to unreliable or misleading results.
The Relative Rate Index (RRI) is a tool used to compare the rates at which different groups experience incarceration, parole eligibility, or other criminal justice system outcomes. In this report, we use the RRI to highlight disparities in parole release and incarceration across race, ethnicity, and sex. An RRI greater than one means that the group experiences the event at a higher rate, while an RRI less than one means they experience it at a lower rate.
State data broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. The U.S. Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

title: North Dakota
text:
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In North Dakota, most people who are incarcerated are eligible for discretionary parole release soon after they enter prison. The parole board has broad discretion to set minimum terms to parole release consideration for each prisoner individually. Violent offenses are subject to an 85-percent eligibility rule.¹

Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
Most prison sentences in New Hampshire include judicial minimum and maximum terms. Judicial minimum term may not exceed one-half of the maximum term.¹







In 2019, 41 percent of people in prison were currently past their parole eligibility. Another 59 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2019.





From 2011 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 15 percent.












Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2019, 91 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic.






In 2019, 92 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.






In 2019, 29 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 35 and 44 years old.














In 2019, 57 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 43 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for rape or sexual assault (32%) and property (21%) offenses.











In 2019, 32 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 and 24.9 years.











Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (50 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 50 for those whose most serious offense is violent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Data is excluded for years where more than 10 percent of the relevant cases are missing the admission year or the max. sentence length variables, and for years when more than 40 percent of the relevant cases areare missing PEY. Relevant cases are all except those with life sentences, sentences of less than a year, and admission types that are not new court commitments or not available. All figures are calculated among relevant cases only.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. Interpret small numbers with caution, as they may introduce variability and lead to less reliable estimates. We do not report on people of “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” because the numbers are generally smaller, and disparities analyses require sufficient sample sizes to produce meaningful comparisons. In the NCRP data, people of “Other race(s)” may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Due to the small numbers in these groups, reporting could lead to unreliable or misleading results.
The Relative Rate Index (RRI) is a tool used to compare the rates at which different groups experience incarceration, parole eligibility, or other criminal justice system outcomes. In this report, we use the RRI to highlight disparities in parole release and incarceration across race, ethnicity, and sex. An RRI greater than one means that the group experiences the event at a higher rate, while an RRI less than one means they experience it at a lower rate.
State data broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. The U.S. Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

title: New Hampshire
text:
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
Most prison sentences in New Hampshire include judicial minimum and maximum terms. Judicial minimum term may not exceed one-half of the maximum term.¹

title: New Hampshire
text:
In 2019, 41 percent of people in prison were currently past their parole eligibility. Another 59 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2019.





From 2011 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 15 percent.

title: New Hampshire
text:
Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2019, 91 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic.






In 2019, 92 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.






In 2019, 29 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 35 and 44 years old.

title: New Hampshire
text: In 2019, 57 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 43 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for rape or sexual assault (32%) and property (21%) offenses.

title: New Hampshire
text: In 2019, 32 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 and 24.9 years.

title: New Hampshire
text: Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (50 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 50 for those whose most serious offense is violent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Data is excluded for years where more than 10 percent of the relevant cases are missing the admission year or the max. sentence length variables, and for years when more than 40 percent of the relevant cases areare missing PEY. Relevant cases are all except those with life sentences, sentences of less than a year, and admission types that are not new court commitments or not available. All figures are calculated among relevant cases only.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Nebraska, prison sentences with maximum terms of four years or less have no parole release eligibility. Maximum terms of 20 years or more almost always include parole-release eligibility, with reductions for good time credits; this prison population becomes eligible at the 50-percent mark of the judicial maximum sentence or earlier.¹







In 2019, 7 percent of people in prison were currently past their parole eligibility. Another 93 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2019.





From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility decreased by 21 percent.

In 2019, 75 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 25 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for rape or sexual assault (36%) and robbery (16%) offenses.











In 2019, 66 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between >=25 years and NA.











Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (100 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 50 for those whose most serious offense is violent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Data is excluded for years where more than 10 percent of the relevant cases are missing the admission year or the max. sentence length variables, and for years when more than 40 percent of the relevant cases areare missing PEY. Relevant cases are all except those with life sentences, sentences of less than a year, and admission types that are not new court commitments or not available. All figures are calculated among relevant cases only.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (100 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 50 for those whose most serious offense is violent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Data is excluded for years where more than 10 percent of the relevant cases are missing the admission year or the max. sentence length variables, and for years when more than 40 percent of the relevant cases areare missing PEY. Relevant cases are all except those with life sentences, sentences of less than a year, and admission types that are not new court commitments or not available. All figures are calculated among relevant cases only.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

title: Nebraska
text:
Interpret small numbers with caution, as they may introduce variability and lead to less reliable estimates. We do not report on people of “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” because the numbers are generally smaller, and disparities analyses require sufficient sample sizes to produce meaningful comparisons. In the NCRP data, people of “Other race(s)” may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Due to the small numbers in these groups, reporting could lead to unreliable or misleading results.
The Relative Rate Index (RRI) is a tool used to compare the rates at which different groups experience incarceration, parole eligibility, or other criminal justice system outcomes. In this report, we use the RRI to highlight disparities in parole release and incarceration across race, ethnicity, and sex. An RRI greater than one means that the group experiences the event at a higher rate, while an RRI less than one means they experience it at a lower rate.
State data broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. The U.S. Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

title: Missouri
text:

*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and excludes people with life sentences and sentences less than one year.


Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Missouri, minimum terms to parole-release eligibility for most people who are incarcerated are established by regulations by the parole board, with a range of 15–33 percent of the maximum sentence. By statute, dangerous offenses serve 85 percent of the maximum to be eligible, and repeated commitments to prison for other serious offenses result in increasing minimums of 40, 50, and 70 percent.¹







In 2019, 50 percent of people in prison were currently past their parole eligibility. Another 38 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2019.





From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 3 percent.












Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2019, 65 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic.






In 2019, 93 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.






In 2019, 33 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old.














In 2019, 61 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 39 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for rape or sexual assault (23%) and property (16%) offenses.











In 2019, 51 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 and 24.9 years.











Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (25 percent for most people, and 85 percent for those with an offense type that might have special eligibility restrictions). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴
